title: Cat Eye Swollen Shut and Weeping: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's eye swollen and weeping? This guide explains the causes, high urgency level, and why a photo is vital for veterinary triage.
content_html:
What is causing my cat's eye to swell and weep?

When a cat's eye is swollen shut and producing discharge, it is a sign of significant pain and inflammation. This condition can be caused by various factors, including corneal ulcers (scratches on the eye surface), viral infections like Feline Herpesvirus, or bacterial conjunctivitis. It could also result from a foreign object, such as a grass seed, trapped behind the eyelid, or trauma from a fight with another animal.

Is this a medical emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Any time a cat is unable or unwilling to open its eye, it should be considered a medical emergency. Eye issues can deteriorate with extreme speed; a minor scratch can lead to a ruptured globe or permanent blindness within 24 to 48 hours. If you see yellow or green discharge, cloudiness, or if your cat is pawing at the eye, please contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

Why a photo helps with triage

In a triage situation, a clear, well-lit photo of the affected eye provides vital information to the veterinary team. A photo helps the professional determine the type of discharge, the severity of the inflammation, and whether there is visible trauma to the cornea. This visual evidence allows the clinic to prioritize your cat's appointment and prepare the necessary diagnostic tools before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Can I wait until tomorrow to see a vet?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to wait. Eye conditions are intensely painful and can lead to permanent vision loss very quickly. Immediate professional assessment is the safest course of action.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No. Many human medications contain ingredients that are toxic to cats or can cause a corneal ulcer to worsen. Never apply any medication to a cat's eye unless specifically directed by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: How can I comfort my cat until we reach the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat in a dim, quiet room to reduce light sensitivity. If you have an Elizabethan collar (cone), put it on immediately to prevent your cat from pawing at and further damaging the eye.
